title: UK Parliament Launches Formal Inquiry Into Banking Restrictions on Crypto Firms
dek: The UK's Crypto and Digital Assets All-Party Parliamentary Group has launched a six-week inquiry into systematic debanking of crypto businesses, citing consistent reports of account denials and transaction restrictions by major banks. A findings report will follow.
author: Crypto Cronkite
key_fact: The APPG, chaired by former Minister Ed Vaizey and Labour MP Gurinder Singh Josan, is investigating whether banking restrictions are proportionate and what impact they have on consumers, businesses, innovation, and competition.
bottom_line: Watch for the conclusion of the six-week evidence period and publication of the APPG's findings report, which will reveal the scope and intensity of banking restrictions documented by crypto sector participants. The inquiry's impact will depend on whether the UK government adopts its recommendations and whether those recommendations carry regulatory or legislative teeth. The premise invalidates if banks' restrictions are shown to be legally mandated by existing financial regulation rather than discretionary policy.
body: The UK's Crypto and Digital Assets All-Party Parliamentary Group (APPG) has launched a formal inquiry into banks' refusal of accounts and restrictions on payments to crypto firms, according to CoinDesk's reporting. The inquiry was kicked off on Tuesday, July 21, 2026.

The APPG is chaired by Ed Vaizey, a former Minister for the Digital Economy and a member of the House of Lords, and Gurinder Singh Josan, a Labour MP. The group is inviting written evidence from across the banking, payments, fintech, and crypto sectors over a six-week call for evidence, per CoinDesk. The APPG will then publish a report setting out its findings and recommendations to the UK government.

The inquiry will focus on a dearth of bank accounts for crypto businesses, including associated professional services such as insurance, according to CoinDesk's reporting. It will also examine restrictions placed by banks on crypto-related transactions, including blocking payments to certain crypto firms or imposing transfer limits. The group wants to understand how restrictions are being applied, whether they are proportionate, and what impact they have on consumers, businesses, innovation, and competition, per CoinDesk.

Several major UK banks have introduced restrictions on crypto-related payments, according to CoinDesk. The difficulties around attaining banking relationships have made operations hard for crypto players, with systematic debanking of firms and individuals, particularly in the US, referred to as "Operation Chokepoint 2.0," per CoinDesk's reporting.

Ed Vaizey stated, according to CoinDesk: "Over a number of years, the APPG has heard consistent reports from crypto and digital asset businesses that they face difficulties accessing bank accounts and banking services, alongside concerns about restrictions on crypto-related transactions by banks."

The sources do not identify which specific UK banks have introduced restrictions or explain their rationale. Banks may argue they are meeting anti-money laundering, sanctions compliance, or reputational risk requirements under existing financial regulation. The inquiry is investigative in nature and may produce recommendations that lack enforcement power if the government does not act on them.
